sentence.target_text: Marchando un café con leche. ¡Aquí tienes!
Hoy tenemos croissants y tarta de manzana.
Claro, en un momento te lo preparo.
Aquí tienes la cuenta: ocho euros.
Sí, claro. Aquí está el datáfono.
No es necesario, pero se agradece.
Aquí tiene, un kilo justo. Son dos euros.
A un euro y medio el kilo.
Los plátanos están de oferta hoy.
Es de piel buena, pero puedo bajar algo.
Está bien, se lo dejo en veinticinco.
Veinte no puedo… veintidós y es suyo.
Gracias. Su puerta es la B12, embarque a las diez.
Siga las señales hasta la zona B.
Todo recto y la segunda a la izquierda.
No, a unos diez minutos a pie.
De siete a diez, en la planta baja.
Sí, la contraseña está en su llave.
De aquí, de toda la vida.
La paella está muy buena hoy.
La azul es muy bonita. ¿Se la lleva?

gloss.text: How do you say "hello" in Spanish?
Translate to Spanish: "She reads a book"
Translate to Spanish: "How much is it?"
Un café con leche, por favor
A greeting plus a friendly question — exactly how you answer a hello.
Buenas noches is for night-time — this is a morning greeting.
Adiós says goodbye — Marta just said hello.
Short and polite — the standard reply to ¿y tú?
That answers nothing here — she asked how you are.
That's what a waiter says to take an order, not a reply to a greeting.
Ask for your drink the polite way
Por favor makes the order polite — the natural way to ask.
Un café con leche, por favor.
Dame with no por favor sounds like a command — too blunt for a café.
A greeting, not an order — and the wrong time of day.
Decline politely and ask the price — two useful phrases together.
Just 'no' sounds curt — add gracias to keep it friendly.
That mixes words up — it says the coffee is a book.
Order with numbers and thank the baker
Number + noun + por favor — a complete, polite order.
Rápido makes it rude — drop it and add por favor.
Asking for a table doesn't order bread.
Politely close the order and ask the total.
Numbers alone don't answer ¿algo más? — say no, gracias or order something.
Bread isn't blue — that names a colour instead of answering.
Choose a shirt using la and el correctly
Camisa is feminine — la camisa azul is right.
Camisa is feminine: la camisa, not el camisa.
The extra es breaks the sentence — prefiero la camisa azul is complete.
No, prefiero la camisa azul es.
Confirm with the article and colour — clear and polite.
Sí, la camisa azul, por favor.
Wrong article and word order — colour follows the noun: la camisa azul.
That says good night instead of confirming the shirt.
Handle euros with the numbers you know
Repeat the number as you hand it over — clear and natural.
Rojo is a colour — the price needs a number: cuatro euros.
He just told you the price — cuatro euros.
Cinco needs a plural: cinco bolsas — and you only need one!
Talking about reading doesn't answer the question about a bag.
Use el and la with the right objects
Libro is masculine — el libro rojo.
Libro is masculine: el libro, not la libro.
The words are shuffled — article first: el libro rojo, por favor.
Mesa is feminine — la mesa verde.
Sí, la mesa verde, por favor.
Mesa is feminine: la mesa, not el mesa.
Sí, el mesa verde, por favor.
Scrambled — keep it as la mesa verde, por favor.
Sí, verde la mesa favor por.
Use the simple present for daily actions
Simple present, first person: como, bebo.
Those are infinitives — conjugate them: como, bebo.
Mañana means tomorrow — the question is about your usual morning.
Third person: ella lee — the -e ending marks she.
Leer is the infinitive — with ella it is lee.
The question is about your sister — use ella, not yo.
Ask basic questions to get to know her
The natural first question — ¿cómo te llamas?
That asks a price — not a way to meet someone.
Missing the question word and marks — ask ¿cómo te llamas?
Answer, then bounce the question back with ¿y tú?
Asking where the bread is changes the subject entirely.
¿Cuánto cuestan...? asks the price of plural things.
That asks his age — not the price of fruit!
Question word first, with marks: ¿cuánto cuestan las manzanas?
Quantity + por favor — a complete polite request.
¿Y tú? bounces a personal question — it does not order fruit.
Articles alone do not make a request — say un kilo, por favor.
Answer in the present and ask why — natural conversation flow.
Saturday is not a colour — answer with your plans.
Accept and ask the time — ¿a qué hora?
The book's price has nothing to do with the movie plan.
It is a question — it needs the marks: ¿a qué hora?
First-person present: como, bebo — a full answer about habits.
Como pan con queso y bebo agua.
Ayer means yesterday — normalmente wants the present: como pan.
Conjugate comer: como pan y bebo agua.
Third person él come + a time phrase — perfect habit talk.
Sí, él come pescado los viernes.
Comes is the tú form — with él it is come.
She asked about your brother — answer with él, not yo.
Find a book at the library
¿Dónde está...? + el libro (masculine) — the right question.
Libro is masculine: el libro azul.
Put the question word first, with marks: ¿dónde está el libro azul?
Sí, el libro azul está dónde.
A polite thanks plus a yes/no question in the present.
As a question it needs marks: ¿abre la biblioteca mañana?
